Eugene Ammons  (1925-04-14)April 14, 1925  Chicago, Illinois, United States

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Gene+Ammons%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Gene+Ammons%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Gene+Ammons%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Gene+Ammons%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(March 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

"**Jug**" **Ammons** (April 14, 1925 – August 6, 1974),[[1]](#cite_note-Dead-1) also known as "The Boss", was an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") tenor saxophonist.[[2]](#cite_note-RCJE-2) The son of [boogie-woogie](/wiki/Boogie-woogie "Boogie-woogie") pianist [Albert Ammons](/wiki/Albert_Ammons "Albert Ammons"),[[1]](#cite_note-Dead-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Rosenthal-3) Gene Ammons is remembered for his accessible music, steeped in soul and R&B.[[4]](#cite_note-LarkinJazz-4)

American tenor saxophonist (1925–1974)

Musical artist

**Eugene** "**Jug**" **Ammons** (April 14, 1925 – August 6, 1974),[[1]](#cite_note-Dead-1) also known as "The Boss", was an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") tenor saxophonist.[[2]](#cite_note-RCJE-2) The son of [boogie-woogie](/wiki/Boogie-woogie "Boogie-woogie") pianist [Albert Ammons](/wiki/Albert_Ammons "Albert Ammons"),[[1]](#cite_note-Dead-1)[[3]](#cite_note-Rosenthal-3) Gene Ammons is remembered for his accessible music, steeped in soul and R&B.[[4]](#cite_note-LarkinJazz-4)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ammons&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Born in Chicago, Illinois,[[4]](#cite_note-LarkinJazz-4) Ammons studied music with instructor [Walter Dyett](/wiki/Walter_Dyett "Walter Dyett") at [DuSable High School](/wiki/DuSable_High_School "DuSable High School"). Ammons began to gain recognition while still at high school when in 1943, at the age of 18, he went on the road with trumpeter [King Kolax](/wiki/King_Kolax "King Kolax")'s band. In 1944, he joined the band of [Billy Eckstine](/wiki/Billy_Eckstine "Billy Eckstine") (who bestowed on him the nickname "Jug" when straw hats ordered for the band did not fit), playing alongside [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker") and later [Dexter Gordon](/wiki/Dexter_Gordon "Dexter Gordon").[[4]](#cite_note-LarkinJazz-4) Performances from this period include "Blowin' the Blues Away," featuring a saxophone duel between Ammons and Gordon. After 1947, when Eckstine became a solo performer, Ammons then led a group, including [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") and [Sonny Stitt](/wiki/Sonny_Stitt "Sonny Stitt"), that performed at Chicago's Jumptown Club. In 1949, Ammons replaced [Stan Getz](/wiki/Stan_Getz "Stan Getz") as a member of [Woody Herman](/wiki/Woody_Herman "Woody Herman")'s Second Herd,[[1]](#cite_note-Dead-1) and then in 1950 formed a duet with Sonny Stitt.[[4]](#cite_note-LarkinJazz-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Grave_of_Eugene_Ammons_%281925%E2%80%931974%29_at_Lincoln_Cemetery%2C_Blue_Island%2C_IL_1.jpg/250px-Grave_of_Eugene_Ammons_%281925%E2%80%931974%29_at_Lincoln_Cemetery%2C_Blue_Island%2C_IL_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grave_of_Eugene_Ammons_(1925%E2%80%931974)_at_Lincoln_Cemetery,_Blue_Island,_IL_1.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Grave_of_Eugene_Ammons_%281925%E2%80%931974%29_at_Lincoln_Cemetery%2C_Blue_Island%2C_IL_2.jpg/250px-Grave_of_Eugene_Ammons_%281925%E2%80%931974%29_at_Lincoln_Cemetery%2C_Blue_Island%2C_IL_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grave_of_Eugene_Ammons_(1925%E2%80%931974)_at_Lincoln_Cemetery,_Blue_Island,_IL_2.jpg)

Two stones at Ammons' grave in Lincoln Cemetery

The 1950s were a prolific period for Ammons and produced some acclaimed recordings such as *[The Happy Blues](/wiki/The_Happy_Blues "The Happy Blues")* (1956). Musicians who played in his groups, apart from Stitt, included [Donald Byrd](/wiki/Donald_Byrd "Donald Byrd"), [Jackie McLean](/wiki/Jackie_McLean "Jackie McLean"), [John Coltrane](/wiki/John_Coltrane "John Coltrane"), [Kenny Burrell](/wiki/Kenny_Burrell "Kenny Burrell"), [Mal Waldron](/wiki/Mal_Waldron "Mal Waldron"), [Art Farmer](/wiki/Art_Farmer "Art Farmer"), and [Duke Jordan](/wiki/Duke_Jordan "Duke Jordan").

His later career was interrupted by two prison sentences for narcotics possession, the first from 1958 to 1960, the second from 1962 to 1969.[[4]](#cite_note-LarkinJazz-4) He recorded as a leader for [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_Records "Mercury Records") (1947–1949), [Aristocrat](/wiki/Aristocrat_Records "Aristocrat Records") (1948–1950), [Chess](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") (1950–1951), [Prestige](/wiki/Prestige_Records "Prestige Records") (1950–1952), [Decca](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records") (1952), and [United](/wiki/United_Records "United Records") (1952–1953). For the rest of his career, he was affiliated with Prestige. After his release from prison in 1969, having served a seven-year sentence at [Joliet](/wiki/Joliet_Prison "Joliet Prison") penitentiary, he signed the largest contract ever offered at that time by Prestige's [Bob Weinstock](/wiki/Bob_Weinstock "Bob Weinstock").

Ammons had the first of two records released by [Leonard Chess](/wiki/Leonard_Chess "Leonard Chess") on the newly-formed [Chess Records](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") label in 1950, titled "My Foolish Heart" (Chess 1425); [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters") was the second record, "Rolling Stone" (Chess 1426). Both records were released simultaneously.

Ammons died in Chicago on August 6, 1974, at the age of 49, from [bone cancer](/wiki/Bone_cancer "Bone cancer") and pneumonia.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) He was buried at [Lincoln Cemetery](/wiki/Lincoln_Cemetery_(Cook_County) "Lincoln Cemetery (Cook County)") in Blue Island, Illinois.

## Playing style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ammons&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Playing style")]

Ammons and [Von Freeman](/wiki/Von_Freeman "Von Freeman") were the founders of the Chicago school of tenor saxophone. Ammons's playing style showed influences from [Lester Young](/wiki/Lester_Young "Lester Young") as well as [Ben Webster](/wiki/Ben_Webster "Ben Webster"). These artists had helped develop the sound of the tenor saxophone to higher levels of expressiveness. Ammons, together with Dexter Gordon and Sonny Stitt, helped integrate their developments with the emerging "vernacular" of the bebop movement, and the chromaticism and rhythmic variety of [Charlie Parker](/wiki/Charlie_Parker "Charlie Parker") is evident in his playing.

While adept at the technical aspects of bebop, in particular its love of harmonic substitutions, Ammons stayed in touch with the commercial blues and R&B of his day. For example, in 1950 the saxophonist's recording of "[My Foolish Heart](/wiki/My_Foolish_Heart_(song) "My Foolish Heart (song)")" made [*Billboard* Magazine's](/wiki/Billboard_magazine "Billboard magazine") black pop charts.[[3]](#cite_note-Rosenthal-3) The [soul jazz](/wiki/Soul_jazz "Soul jazz") movement of the mid-1960s, often using the combination of tenor saxophone and [Hammond B3 electric organ](/wiki/Hammond_organ "Hammond organ"), counts him as a founder. With a thicker, warmer tone than Stitt or Gordon, Ammons could at will exploit a vast range of textures on the instrument, vocalizing it in ways that anticipated later artists such as [Stanley Turrentine](/wiki/Stanley_Turrentine "Stanley Turrentine"), [Houston Person](/wiki/Houston_Person "Houston Person"), and even [Archie Shepp](/wiki/Archie_Shepp "Archie Shepp"). Ammons showed little interest, however, in the modal jazz of John Coltrane, [Joe Henderson](/wiki/Joe_Henderson "Joe Henderson") or [Wayne Shorter](/wiki/Wayne_Shorter "Wayne Shorter") that was emerging at the same time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Discography

* *[Gene Ammons in Sweden](/wiki/Gene_Ammons_in_Sweden "Gene Ammons in Sweden")* ([Enja](/wiki/Enja_Records "Enja Records") 3093, 1973 [1981])
* *[Brasswind](/wiki/Brasswind "Brasswind")* (Prestige 10080, 1974)
* *[Goodbye](/wiki/Goodbye_(Gene_Ammons_album) "Goodbye (Gene Ammons album)")* (Prestige 10093, 1974 [1975])
* *Swinging the Jugg* (Roots 1002, 1970 [1976])

**As co-leader with [Sonny Stitt](/wiki/Sonny_Stitt "Sonny Stitt")**

* *[Kaleidoscope](/wiki/Kaleidoscope_(Sonny_Stitt_album) "Kaleidoscope (Sonny Stitt album)")* (Prestige 7077, 1950–52 [1957])
* *[Boss Tenors](/wiki/Boss_Tenors "Boss Tenors"): Straight Ahead from Chicago – August 1961* ([Verve](/wiki/Verve_Records "Verve Records") 8426, 1962)
* *[Boss Tenors in Orbit!](/wiki/Boss_Tenors_in_Orbit! "Boss Tenors in Orbit!")* (Verve 8468, 1962)
* *[Dig Him!](/wiki/Dig_Him! "Dig Him!")* (Argo 697, 1962) reissued as *We'll Be Together Again* (Prestige 7606, 1969)
* *[Soul Summit](/wiki/Soul_Summit "Soul Summit")* (Prestige 7234, 1962)
* *[You Talk That Talk!](/wiki/You_Talk_That_Talk! "You Talk That Talk!")* (Prestige 10019, 1971)
* *[God Bless Jug and Sonny](/wiki/God_Bless_Jug_and_Sonny "God Bless Jug and Sonny")* [live] (Prestige 11019, 1973 [2001])
* *[Left Bank Encores](/wiki/Left_Bank_Encores "Left Bank Encores")* [live] (Prestige 11022, 1973 [2002])
* *[Together Again for the Last Time](/wiki/Together_Again_for_the_Last_Time "Together Again for the Last Time")* (Prestige 10100, 1973 [1976])

### As sideman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gene_Ammons&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: As sideman")]

**With [Bennie Green](/wiki/Bennie_Green "Bennie Green")**

* *[Soul Stirrin'](/wiki/Soul_Stirrin%27 "Soul Stirrin'")* (Blue Note, 1958)
* *[The Swingin'est](/wiki/The_Swingin%27est "The Swingin'est")* (Vee Jay, 1959)

**With [Richard "Groove" Holmes](/wiki/Richard_Holmes_(organist) "Richard Holmes (organist)")**

* *[Groovin' with Jug](/wiki/Groovin%27_with_Jug "Groovin' with Jug")* (Pacific Jazz, 1961)
* *[Tell It Like It Is](/wiki/Tell_It_Like_It_Tis "Tell It Like It Tis")* (Pacific Jazz, 1966)

**With [Howard McGhee](/wiki/Howard_McGhee "Howard McGhee")**

* *House Warmin'!* (Argo, 1963) – originally issued in 1962 on Winley Records as *Nothin' But Soul* under Gene Ammons' name.
* *Maggie: The Savoy Sessions* (Savoy, 1977)[2LP] – compilation. rec. 1948

**With [Jack McDuff](/wiki/Jack_McDuff "Jack McDuff")**

* *[Brother Jack Meets the Boss](/wiki/Brother_Jack_Meets_the_Boss "Brother Jack Meets the Boss")* (Prestige, 1962)
* *Rock Candy* (Prestige, 1972)[2LP] – compilation

**With others**

* [David Axelrod](/wiki/David_Axelrod_(musician) "David Axelrod (musician)"), *Heavy Axe* (Fantasy, 1974)
* [Richard B. Boone](/wiki/Richard_B._Boone "Richard B. Boone"), *I've Got a Right to Sing* (Nocturne, 1970)
* [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis"), *Bopping the Blues* (Black Lion, 1987) – rec. 1946
* [Billy Eckstine](/wiki/Billy_Eckstine "Billy Eckstine"), *The Legendary Big Band* (Savoy, 2002)[2CD] – anthology (all of Eckstine's recordings for the DeLuxe and National labels). rec. 1944–1947.
* [Charles Mingus](/wiki/Charles_Mingus "Charles Mingus"), *[Charles Mingus and Friends in Concert](/wiki/Charles_Mingus_and_Friends_in_Concert "Charles Mingus and Friends in Concert")* (Columbia, 1972)
* [Andrew White](/wiki/Andrew_White_(saxophonist) "Andrew White (saxophonist)"), *Red Top* (Andrew's Music, 1977)
